title: Saving the Civil Service Act
plain_summary: This bill stops the government from creating Schedule F jobs, which are a type of government position that can be easily fired. It makes sure that most government workers keep their job protections and cannot be moved to different types of positions without agreeing to it first. The bill also limits how many workers can be moved from protected jobs to unprotected jobs each time there is a new president. This protects government employees from being fired just because of their political beliefs.
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Mr. Walkinshaw asked unanimous consent that he may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 492, a bill originally introduced by Representative Connolly, for t
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving the Civil Service Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Limitations on Excepting Positions from Competitive Service and Transferring Positions
sections.text: This section sets rules about how government jobs can be moved between different hiring systems. The competitive service means jobs that are filled through fair hiring processes like tests or open competition. The excepted service means jobs that can be filled without this competition. A government job that is normally hired through competition can only be moved to the excepted service if it goes into one of the existing schedules A through E that were in place on September thirtieth two thousand twenty. The same rules and conditions that were in effect on that date must also be followed. Jobs already in the excepted service can only be moved to these same approved schedules. If a government agency wants to move a job that someone is already working in to schedule C of the excepted service, they must get permission from the Director of the Office of Personnel Management first. Schedule C jobs are typically political appointments. During any four year presidential term, an agency cannot move more than one percent of its total employees from competitive service jobs to excepted service jobs. However, they can always move at least five employees even if that is more than one percent. No employee can be moved from their current job type to a different schedule without their written permission. This applies whether they are moving within the excepted service or from competitive service to excepted service. These rules also apply to jobs at the Department of Veterans Affairs even though they normally have different rules. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management must create regulations to carry out this section. The section defines agency as any department, agency, or part of the Federal Government. Competitive service and excepted service have the meanings given in existing federal law. Director means the Director of the Office of Personnel Management.
